published.text:
### Hero
Heading: What to Automate First for Home Inspectors
Body: If your home inspection business knows it needs better automation, the first move usually should not be a giant all-in-one system. It should be one bounded workflow that fixes the leak already costing you referrals, bookings, or follow-through. For most home inspectors, that first workflow is one of four things: lead follow-up on new agent and buyer inquiries, missed-call recovery while you are still on-site, inspection scheduling and reminders once the booking is real, or report-delivery follow-up after the inspection is done. The right choice depends on where the business is already losing momentum today — before contact happens, while the phone is unanswered, during booking friction, or after the field work is complete.
Sub: Below: how to choose the first home-inspector workflow to automate, how this page stays distinct from the broader home-inspector page and the narrower child pages already live, what adjacent proof honestly supports the recommendation, and when you should keep the first project smaller instead of forcing a bigger operating-system build too early.

### Core sections
- The first home-inspector workflows usually worth automating
- Which home-inspector workflow should you automate first?
- This page vs. the rest of the home-inspector cluster
- Is this a good fit for your inspection business?
- Relevant proof and adjacent proof

### FAQ
Subhead: Practical questions about choosing the first home-inspector automation
Topics: what should come first, how this differs from the broader home-inspector page, when missed-call recovery should beat lead follow-up, when scheduling should come before another front-end workflow, and when report-delivery closeout is a valid first project.

### Final CTA
Heading: Need help choosing the first home-inspector workflow to automate?
Body: Book a 30-minute call. We will look at where your business is leaking referrals, missed calls, booking momentum, or post-inspection follow-through now, then recommend whether the first move should be lead follow-up, missed-call recovery, scheduling, or report-delivery closeout before anything broader gets built.

edits.description: Shipped after broad reconciliation across `GEO-PLAN.md`, `docs/geo-roadmap.md`, `docs/programmatic-seo-pattern-matrix.md`, `docs/geo-plan-2026-03.md`, live routes/pages, and `scripts/geo-prompts.json`.

Chosen because the live home-inspector cluster already had the broad parent plus narrow child pages for early-funnel response, scheduling, post-inspection closeout, and the phone layer, but still had no page helping an owner choose the first bounded workflow.
The page stays distinct by framing the buyer decision one step earlier than the existing child pages instead of rehashing any single workflow.

Proof framing stays honest: the broader home-inspector page, the restaurant voice-agent case study, and the published e-commerce CRM case study support the workflow pattern without pretending there is already a home-inspector-specific first-project case study.
